title: Commercial Roofing in Fairacres, NM | Commercial Roofing Contractors of Las Cruces
metaDescription: Roofing for pecan orchard packing sheds, farm stands, ag equipment dealers, and rural outbuildings in Fairacres, NM, on the west side of the Mesilla Valley.

html:
Orchard-Country Roof Work West of the Mesilla Valley

Fairacres is pecan country, rows of trees running down toward the valley floor, small farm operations, nurseries, and rural residential lots mixed in with the orchards. There isn't a commercial strip here in the way there is closer to downtown Las Cruces. What there is: packing sheds, farm stands, equipment barns, and a scattering of small ag-related businesses that need the same roof attention as any building in the valley, just spread across bigger lots.

Farm Stands and Small Ag Businesses

Roadside farm stands and nurseries in Fairacres run simple, low-cost roof structures, often metal panel over a pole frame, sometimes an older flat-roof section on a building that started as something else and got repurposed for retail. These owners are usually running the business themselves, so a roof call has to work around their hours rather than the other way around. We keep repairs practical here: patch what can be patched, and be straight about when a small operation is better off replacing a section outright instead of paying for repeated spot fixes.

Pecan Processing and Cold Storage

Pecan operations that process or store their own crop run cold storage and processing buildings with roofs that can't afford a leak during harvest. Most of what we see is exposed-fastener metal on newer storage additions and older built-up sections on structures that have been standing longer than the current owner has run the operation. Flashing at equipment penetrations and rooftop unit curbs is where we spend the most time on these buildings, since that's where harvest-season leaks tend to start.

Timing matters more here than almost anywhere else in the valley. A processing building down for roof repair during harvest is lost capacity the operation can't easily make up, so we plan non-emergency work for the off-season whenever an owner can wait, and move fast when they can't.

Horse Properties and Rural Outbuildings

A good share of Fairacres is rural residential with horse properties mixed among the orchards, barns, hay storage, and equipment sheds that get less attention than a commercial building but still need a sound roof to protect feed and equipment. These are almost always straightforward metal panel roofs, and the main failure points are fastener backout and rust starting at cut edges where panels were trimmed on site without proper sealing.

Farm Equipment Dealers and Ag Supply Buildings

A handful of farm equipment dealers and ag supply operations sit along the main roads through Fairacres, serving the orchard and row-crop operations spread across the west side of the valley. These buildings tend to combine a retail counter or office up front with a much larger service bay and parts storage area behind it, and the roof over that back section sees a different kind of wear: welding sparks, equipment exhaust heat, and more roof traffic from technicians accessing rooftop units than a typical storage building would ever get. We look closely at penetration flashing and walk-pad wear on these roofs, since foot traffic during service bay maintenance breaks down membrane surfaces faster than weather alone.

Parts storage in particular needs a roof that won't compromise inventory sitting under it for months at a time. A slow leak that would just mean a wet floor in an open bay can mean a warranty claim on stored parts if it isn't caught early, so we treat these buildings with the same attention we'd give a cold storage roof even though the contents are steel and rubber instead of produce.

Dust From Orchard Operations

Orchard floor maintenance and irrigation cycling kick up more dust in Fairacres than you'd get on a paved commercial lot, and that dust settles into every low point and drain on a flat roof. On the packing and storage buildings that do have low-slope sections, we check and clear drains as a standard part of any service visit rather than waiting for a customer to report ponding, because by the time water is standing visibly, the drain has usually been partially blocked for a while.

  • Fastener backout at cut panel edges on barns and equipment sheds
  • Dust-clogged drains on low-slope packing and storage roofs
  • Flashing failure at rooftop unit curbs on processing buildings
  • Rust starting at unsealed field-cut metal panel edges
  • Sealant failure around repurposed retail building penetrations
  • Ponding at low points where drainage slope has settled over time

What Fairacres Property Owners Ask Us

Do you work on horse barns and ag outbuildings, or just commercial roofs?

We work on both. A hay barn roof failure is just as much a problem as a commercial roof leak if it's letting weather into stored feed or equipment, and the repair approach for metal panel roofing is largely the same either way.

Can you schedule around our harvest or growing season?

We plan non-urgent work around your calendar whenever we can, especially for packing and cold storage buildings where downtime during harvest actually costs the operation. Active leaks get prioritized regardless of season.

Is dust actually a serious problem for flat roofs out here?

It contributes to drain blockage more than most owners expect. Orchard dust settles into low points and strainers, and a partially blocked drain can lead to ponding that shortens the life of the membrane around it, so we check drains on every visit.

What's the most common roof type you see in Fairacres?

Exposed-fastener metal panel, by a wide margin, on barns, equipment sheds, and most newer storage additions. Older built-up roofing shows up on some of the longer-standing packing and processing structures.

Do you charge more to come out this far from town?

No separate travel charge for Fairacres. It's a short drive from Las Cruces and part of our normal service area.
